caveats: a: The BLS source is much broader than the industry.
a: The code spans highly automated volume producers and labor-intensive custom batch shops.
rho: Realization depends on digitized product, tooling, and customer-specification data.
rho: Regulated and specification-critical work still requires human approval.
e: Public company descriptions do not establish EBITDA-band eligibility.
e: The frozen firm count is modeled from size classes rather than observed transaction candidates.
s5: Establishments are not equivalent to firms.
s5: Broad manufacturing sales include less specialized businesses.
q: Public pricing claims do not reveal renewal behavior.
q: Retention varies sharply by compliance and customization intensity.
d5: Trade data describe downstream textiles and apparel, not the component industry directly.
d5: End-market mix can make a target materially more resilient or more exposed than the industry range.
o: Some sales and art-support work may become self-service.
o: Demand loss from substitution or offshoring is captured mainly in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is primarily repeat business-to-business component supply through standard catalog orders, custom specifications, private-label or contract runs, and attaching-machine tooling or support; a smaller promotional segment uses digitally configured, made-to-order batch pricing.
lens.excluded_activities: Precious-metal jewelry fasteners, hypodermic and suture needles, phonograph styli, distributors without manufacturing, captive internal plants, consumer craft retailers without repeat manufacturing customers, and platform-scale firms outside the lower middle market.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of non-precious fasteners, buttons, needles, pins, buckles, eyelets, grommets, snaps, and related attaching components supplied repeatedly to apparel, medical, military, marine, industrial, promotional, and other external customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover all miscellaneous manufacturing, not six-digit 339993. I map office, management, sales, business, design, quoting, customer support, scheduling, procurement, quality-document, and information-heavy production-support tasks into partial AI exposure while leaving high-speed forming, stamping, plating, assembly, inspection, packing, and material movement mostly unexposed. The broad group includes medical-device work with more technical staff, so it may overstate exposure for commodity notions manufacturing.
caveats: The BLS source is much broader than the industry.
The code spans highly automated volume producers and labor-intensive custom batch shops.
a.change_evidence: A 339993-specific occupational and payroll mix, supplemented by time studies for order-to-production and quality workflows, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: Production occupations account for nearly half of broad miscellaneous-manufacturing employment, limiting exposure. The opportunity is concentrated in quoting, artwork or design setup, specification review, order entry, production planning, purchasing, documentation, customer service, and sales.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production and OTEXA textile-apparel trade data cover broad customer markets, while manufacturer pages show diversified applications but not a five-year 339993 forecast. I translate soft domestic apparel production and import exposure into downside, offset by medical, military, marine, industrial, replacement, and promotional demand. Broad industrial growth may overstate the outlook for apparel-heavy targets.
caveats: Trade data describe downstream textiles and apparel, not the component industry directly.
End-market mix can make a target materially more resilient or more exposed than the industry range.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year shipment forecast by 339993 product family, paired with each target's domestic-apparel, defense, medical, industrial, and promotional revenue mix, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Fastening functions remain necessary across clothing and industrial products, but U.S. apparel production and import competition pressure domestic volumes. Specification-driven military, medical, safety, marine, and industrial uses diversify demand and can sustain domestic supply.

caveats: Public company descriptions do not establish EBITDA-band eligibility.
The frozen firm count is modeled from size classes rather than observed transaction candidates.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map of customer recurrence, manufacturing versus distribution mix, ownership, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Repeat component demand, catalog replenishment, custom production, and tooling support fit the recurring external-customer lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive plants, hobby or consumer-only models, establishments belonging to large groups, and firms whose economics fall outside the LMM band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 339993 ($339,861,862) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 339993 ($924,157,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $251,266 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 339993); class <5: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $364,711 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $71,556 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,359,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $266,659 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,361,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $463,359 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,203,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,198,053 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,769,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,410,310 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $116,789,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,914,100 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 22

caveats: Some sales and art-support work may become self-service.
Demand loss from substitution or offshoring is captured mainly in d5.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on customer in-house production, additive manufacturing, fastening-free product redesign, and automated digital ordering that bypasses current suppliers would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Buttons, pins, snaps, needles, eyelets, grommets, and buckles remain physical inputs requiring accountable manufacture and quality control. AI can enable self-service design and ordering but cannot replace the component itself as software.

q.bridge: Manufacturer pages show catalog and custom components, competitive or upfront pricing, design support, technical specifications, attaching equipment, and high-volume production, but do not measure retention of AI-created benefits. I infer more capture in custom, compliance-critical, rush, and low-volume work and less in commodity components or large OEM contracts. The proxy likely overstates retention for price-transparent apparel notions.
caveats: Public pricing claims do not reveal renewal behavior.
Retention varies sharply by compliance and customization intensity.
q.change_evidence: Gross-margin changes after workflow savings, separated by standard catalog, custom, promotional, military, medical, and OEM accounts, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Military specifications, medical safety, tooling compatibility, custom design, and reliable domestic supply support differentiation. Commodity buttons, pins, and apparel fasteners face price competition, imports, and procurement rebids, creating substantial pass-through.

caveats: Realization depends on digitized product, tooling, and customer-specification data.
Regulated and specification-critical work still requires human approval.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots at comparable firms covering quote time, art proofing, order entry, customer support, production planning, and document preparation would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, small manufacturers can deploy AI in quoting, digital proofs, specification search, product matching, customer communications, purchasing, scheduling, and quality-document drafting. Implementation is constrained by legacy systems, small data teams, physical inspection, and military, medical, or commercial specification controls.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell measures broad manufacturing transactions and Census owner-characteristics data are economy-wide, not 339993 control transfers. I use them to infer a moderate five-year succession or sale probability and haircut for the industry's 89 employer establishments, corporate ownership, and specialized tooling. Broad marketplace data may overstate qualifying transfer rates for this niche.
caveats: Establishments are not equivalent to firms.
Broad manufacturing sales include less specialized businesses.
s5.change_evidence: A matched ownership and deal database for the 339993 universe, including owner age, corporate affiliation, and recent control transactions, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Small manufacturers do transact and succession creates supply, but the absolute industry is small and includes long-lived strategic operators or establishments that cannot be sold independently.

principal_driver: Repeat orders across large specification catalogs create reusable quote, tooling, design, and customer-history data for AI-enabled operating leverage.
principal_weakness: A tiny establishment base and strong commodity or import pressure limit both target supply and commercial retention.

run_meta.title: Fastener, Button, Needle, and Pin Manufacturing

sources.supports: Provides broad manufacturing closed-transaction and valuation benchmarks for 2021-2025, used only as a transfer proxy.

sources.supports: Provides economy-wide owner-age and ownership-characteristics data used only as a succession proxy.

sources.supports: Reports broad U.S. manufacturing and industrial output conditions used only as a demand proxy for non-apparel end markets.

sources.supports: Provides frequently updated U.S. textile, apparel, footwear, leather, and travel-goods import and export statistics, supporting import-exposure diligence for apparel-linked fasteners.
